source_text.text:
[PDF_PAGE 15 | score=1] the household. This revolved around the hurim, an inviolable sanctuary where a man kept his wives from the lecherous grasp of others. The Shriu had strict laws condemning adulterers: decapitation for the man, death by stoning for the woman. Nevertheless, Mus- lims guarded their wives very carefully, often appointing eunuchs for this duty. The contrast of Western and Arab cultures is one of the strongest and most entertaining possibilities of a Crusades campaign. Over a wries of adventures with Frankish characters, the DM can transform the Saracens from the standard medieval view of Muslims as two- dimensional heathens into believable and interesting adversaries, perhaps even close allies! On
[PDF_PAGE 70 | score=1] ”You must sufer many things in the name of Christ, wretchedness, poverty, nakedness, perse- cution, need, sickness, hunger, thirst, and other - things of this kind.” -Pope Urban 11,1095 laiFM rope Urban I1 launchea the rirst Crusade during the summer of 1095. Five months ear- lier, an envoy of the Emperor Alexius I had arrived in Rome to plead for assistance against the Turks, who were threatening the Byzantine Empire. Urban 11, who had been courting a more open relationship with the Eastern Church for the past 10 years, soon called for a Crusade to liberate both Constan- tinople and the Holy City of Jerusalem from the ”pagan” Muslims. With his first sermon to an assembly of bishops in south
[PDF_PAGE 96 | score=1] Forey, Alan. The Military Orders from the Twelfth to the Early Fourteenth Centuries, University of Toronto Press, Toronto, 1992. An investigation of Military Orders in Europe and Palestine, including organiza- tion, tactics, and regulations. Hallam, Elizabeth (editor). Chronicles of the Crusades, Weidenfeld and Nicolson, New York, 1989. Anthology of primary historical sources, including letters, speeches, and other contemporary texts. Kieckhefer, Richard. Magic in the Middle Ages, Cambridge University Press, Cambridge, 1989. An interesting and often humorous look at the concept of magic in medieval Europe. Lesourd, Paul and Ramiz, Jean Marie. On the Path of the Crusaders, Masada Press, Israe
